title-group.article-title: Autologous Chin Augmentation with Cervico-mental Angle Correction in Burn Scar Contracture

contrib-group.aff: aM.D, Associate Professor, Department of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Zare Hospital, Faculty of Medicine, Mazandaran University of Medical Sciences, Sari, Iran
bM.D, Clinical Research and Development Unit of Bu-Ali Sina Hospital, Mazandaran University of Medical Sciences, Sari, Iran

dMaster of Health Information, Zare Psychiatry and Burn Center, Mazandaran University of Medical Sciences, Sari, Iran
eBiomedical student, Faculty of Science, York University, Toronto, Canada

author-notes.corresp: ⁎Corresponding author: Mohammadhossein Hesamirostami, MD, Associate professor of Plastic and Reconstructive Surgery, Zare Hospital, Km 5, Sari to Neka main road, Sari, Iran, Postal code: 4843185774; hesami_plas@yahoo.com

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.title: Summary
abstract.p: Microgenia, retrogenia, cervico-mental angle obtusity, and lower lip ectropion are complications of severe burn scar contracture and are associated with functional and psychosocial disturbances. These complications can be managed in several ways, for example using fat or dermo-fat flap as autologous tissue transposition appears appropriate for simultaneous chin augmentation with cervico-mental angle improvement. We aimed to delay skin grafting to increase the chance of complete integration.

From November 2015 to February 2023, 12 patients who underwent surgical treatment with fat or dermo-fat flap and delayed skin graft were included in the study. In this prospective study, we measured the cervico-mental and Legan's angles using the Digimizer Software pre- and post-operatively on 8 (7 female, 1 male) eligible patients who signed the informed consent.

Although several methods are available for chin augmentation, transposition of tissues through structural rearrangement to address all the problems appears to be a cost-effective alternative for patients with obtusity due to hypertrophic scar of cervico-mental area with microgenia and retrogenia.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Severe burn scar contracture in the chin and cervico-mental area causes retrogenia and microgenia. Lower lip ectropion and obtusity of the cervico-mental angle (CMA) are also observed in some cases. These complications lead to functional disabilities and aesthetic dissatisfaction, which appears to be more common than previously reported in the literature.1,2 If the complications occur during the growth phase, the final result would be retrogenia and microgenia. Indeed, blunting of the CMA can aggravate the pseudomicrogenia appearance.3 Tightness of the burned or grafted skin that directly correlates with the degree of hypertrophic scarring in the cervico-facial area has a negative impact on chin augmentation by alloplastic material or genioplasty.4, 5, 6 These patients also face psychosocial conflicts due to functional and aesthetic problems.6 As hypertrophic burn scar deformities and contracture of the head and neck after burn accidents are relatively common in these victims, crucial planning for the treatment of these abnormalities is required. Some studies argue that these problems can be managed by applying autologous fat or dermo-fat flaps with good to excellent results.2,7 In the present study, we aimed to report the experience of chin augmentation and the creation of a satisfactory CMA using a surgical method that is similar to the previously mentioned methods, with a little difference and concomitant quantitative measurements.2,7 In the present study, sub-mental autologous tissue was used as a fat or dermo-fat flap for chin augmentation. Simultaneously, CMA correction was carried out after contracture release of neck area. In contrast to similar studies on the coverage of the transposed flap and its donor site, a delaying strategy for skin graft was used. After excising a burn scar or releasing a contracture area, to avoid seroma and hematoma under the graft due to excising a considerable amount of vascular and lymphatic channels in sub-mental area and the necessity of a smoother and vascularized bed for complete taking of the graft, especially thick skin types, delaying appears to be more logical.

sec.title: Patients and Methods
sec.p: Between November 2015 to February 2023, we performed 12 fat or dermo-fat flaps and delay skin graft in the burn and reconstruction section at the University of Zare Hospital. This retrospective analysis on prospective study was approved by the Mazandaran University of Medical Science Research Ethical Committee (IR.MAZUMS.REC.1403.20418)

Adequate explanation was given to all patients about the details of the surgical technique and probable outcomes and complications, and 8 patients who signed informed consent were enrolled in the study. All surgeries were performed by the senior author.

We took preoperative photographs and post-operative photographs 6 months after the surgery with the patients in standing point and looking at a point at their eye level. Using the Digimizer Software (version 5.4.7 medcalc. Software. Ltd.), the CMA in profile view of pre- and post-operative photos were measured and compared.8,9

A sub-mental line was drawn on pre- and post-operative photos, and everywhere the line was broken in an oblique way caudally, thus CMA was revealed and measured. Notably, the post-operative angle was evident (Figure 1,2). The normal and attractive angle of the cervico-mental is approximately 90° to 130°.10 The Legan's angle was also measured pre- and post-operatively to evaluate the amount of soft tissue advancement of the chin. For this purpose, a line was drawn from the Glabella to the sub-nasal that continues caudally, and another line was also drawn from the sub-nasal to pogonion. The mean size of this angle is 12°(8°-16°) in normal cases11 (Figures illustrated in Supplementary Material).Figure 1
A 35-year-old female with second and third-degree cervico-facial burn (flame burn, %29 TBSA) was referred for facial reconstruction 12 years after the burn injury.
A-Pre-op photo of CMA (173.23°), and Legan's angle (16.04°)
B-Post-op photo of CMA (109.97°) and Legan's angle (11.68°)Figure 1Figure 2
A 25-year-old female patient with retrogenia and obtuse cervico-mental angle underwent surgery one year after the burn injury.
A-Pre-op photo of CMA (173.04°) and Legan's angle (19.84°)
B-Post-op photo of CMA (130.91°) and Legan's angle (11.64°)Figure 2

Patients’ variables were summarized using descriptive statistics and presented as frequency and median (min-max). To measure the correction of angles, we used non-parametric Wilcoxon's statistical test and p<0.05 were considered statistically significant. All statistical analyses were conducted using the statistical software IBM version 26.

sec.title: Surgical Technique
sec.p:
In a standing position, with the patient looking forward (as required for taking photographs of the chin area), labio-mental sulcus and hypertrophic scar contracture area for incision or excision were pointed and drawn. After designing, the diluted epinephrine solution (1/200,000) was injected underneath the scar in the cervical area, except for the pedicle of the fat or dermo-fat flap near the chin area. In some cases, the incision was made for release of the contracture and adequate undermining. In other instances, when thick hypertrophic scar was present, excision of the hypertrophied thick scar was made. Then, a triangular shaped tissue composed of fat or dermo-fat as a random flap, based on the caudal border of the chin for rolling and transferring to the anterior border of the chin was dissected. For this purpose, the base of the triangular flap near the caudal border of the chin was marked, and then the tip area of the flap was marked according to the required length. The tip-base distance was determined using a ruler for easy and tension free in-setting of the flap caudal to the labio-mental sulcus (Figure 3A-C). Depth of the dissection in the chin area was the sub-dermal or sub-scar plan, and for sub-mental area after undermining or excising the scar (superficially), it was chosen under the fat of this area with care to not elevate excess tissue. The cephalic limit of undermining was a few mm from cephalic to caudal point of the labio-mental sulcus. In the next stage, the tip of the flap was sutured to the caudal point of the labio-mental sulcus as deep as possible (periosteum) using a 3-0 Vicryl or PDS (Poly Dioxanone Suture, Supa, Medical Devices, Tehran, Iran) to prevent ectropion of the lower lip. The other 2 sutures were put on each side of the central suture, just 8-10 mm postero-lateral and a few mm caudal to it, according to the in-setting of tissues. Any excess tissue that remained caudal and lateral to the flap area was excised with meticulous hemostasis. In some cases, we sutured the anterior edge of platysma in 2 to 3 points and if necessary, the edge of platysma was cut horizontally to create a better shape of the cervico-mental area (Figure 3C arrow) as in aesthetic cervicoplasty cases.12 The remaining elevated skin and coverage tissues of the chin that were retracted for suturing fat or dermo-fat flaps, were returned and sutured to the cervical area's coverage tissue. Otherwise, if the scar on the chin area was removed, we had to place a skin graft on the transposed flap. If there was adequate, good skin on the medial part of the arm, it was the first choice for skin harvesting for grafting on the chin and neck area. If arm skin was not suitable, the skin of the abdomen or thigh was used. To reduce secondary contracture, a thick, split, or full thickness skin graft was preferred. When selecting a skin graft for covering the denuded area, we opted a strategy with a delay of 5-6 days. On an outpatient basis, the wound was dressed daily for 5 or 6 days, then the patients were admitted again, which was followed by harvesting of a thick split or full thick skin graft was from the previously mentioned areas. Skin graft was sutured using 5-0 nylon continuously on the borders and some 4-0 catgut chromic (Supa Medical Devices, Tehran, Iran) in scattered condition in various points as quilting sutures to fix it to the bed, finally it was fixed by tie-over dressing in place (Figure 3d and 4a, 4b).Figure 3
A-Elevated triangular fat flap, A-B pedicle of the flap attached on the sub-mental border, and C tip of the flap.
B-Profile view of the elevated triangular flap
C-The tip of the flap was turned around the chin and sutured to the periosteum on the labio-mental sulcus as deeply as possible.
D-Delayed skin graft, the photo reveals the enhanced labio-mental sulcus, an augmented chin, and a corrected cervico-mental angle.Figure 3Figure 4
A-Pre-operative photo of a 39-year-old woman with evident retrogenia and obtuse cervico-mental angle deformity caused by hypertrophic burn scar contracture. She had been operated on in another center for cervical contracture, which resulted in sub-optimal outcome.
B- Early post-operative photo showing the result after she underwent surgery for correcting all deformities.Figure 4

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Patients’ characteristics are shown in Table 1. In our study, 8 patients (7 female, 1 male) underwent fat or dermo-fat flap and delay skin graft. Local or expanded flap could not be used for contracture release for neck reconstruction because of extensive burn scar in cervical area. The median age at the time of surgery was 36.9 years (range, 25 to 50.11 years). The median age at the time of burn injury was 23.40 years (range, 5 to 48.7 years). The median follow-up period after this operation was 9 months (range, 6 to 42 months). All flaps and grafts survived completely. Hypertrophic scar in suture line occurred in one case that was improved with intra-scar triamcinolone injection.Table 1
Patient characteristicsTable 1
Patients’ informationFindings
Patients, N8
Sex, (%)
Male1 (12.5)
Female7 (87.5)
Diagnosis (%)
Flame burn6 (75)
Chemical burn1 (12.5)
Electrical burn1 (12.5)
Age at time of surgery (years), median (Max-Min)34.9 (50.11–25.00)
Age at time of burn injury (years), median (Max-Min)23.40 (48.70–5.00)
Follow up (months), median (Max-Min)9 (42–6)
Complication (%)0 (0)

The median CMA was 172.71° (range, 156.40° to 178.37°) decreased to 130.37° (range, 109.97° to 142.06°) post-operatively (P=0.012). The median Legan's angle was 13.99° (range, 12.32° to 24.17°) decreased to 10.51° (range, 8.24° to 11.68°) post-operatively (P=0.012; Table 2).Table 2
Surgery characteristicsTable 2
Median (Max − −Min)p-value
CMASig = 0.012
Pre-op172.71 (178.37–156.40)
Post-op130.37 (109.97-142.06)
Legan's angleSig = 0.012
Pre-op13.99 (24.17–12.32)
Post-op10.51 (11.68–8.24)

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: There are 2 known and widely accepted methods for increasing chin projection and creating pleasant chin contour, including genioplasty by alloplastic material and sliding genioplasty by osteotomy.6,11,13, 14, 15 Recently, advancement in the chin augmentation using filler injection, especially in the sub-periosteal layer, was added to our armamentarium.16 These approaches can improve labio-mental and chin contour; however, they have little impact on CMA and no effect on cervical contracture in burn patients, except for the cases that require incision or excision of scar and skin grafting.13 Although these methods are appropriate even for burn patients, the dermo-fat or fat flap has also been introduced as a good alternative surgery with increased advantages. Deformities in the chin and cervico-mental area caused by severe burn scar contracture require excision or release; therefore, using a new, more pliable coverage, such as a skin graft or an expanded flap, is mandatory. Tissue manipulation provides the surgeon with an opportunity to use an easier and cost-effective approach to achieve chin augmentation and create better CMA by fat or dermo-fat flap. There are reports on using fat or dermo-fat flaps from the sub-mental area for chin augmentation.2,7 For coverage of the released or excised area, an expanded flap or skin graft was used. Manafi et al.2 reported that most of their complications were due to patchy, incomplete skin graft adherence. Motamed et al. reported 2 partial graft losses. They had to graft again after conservative management.7 Owing to manipulation and extensive undermining of tissues with rich lymphatic channels and nodes, which raised concerns regarding drainage that could lead to seroma, and based on a prior experience with partial skin graft necrosis caused by hematoma under the graft despite adequate hemostasis, we decided to delay skin grafting by 5-6 days. Another reason for the delay in skin grafting was that thicker skin grafts require a well-vascularized and stable wound bed. Thicker skin graft resists recurrence of contracture. We recommend a tissue expander (TE) and an expanded flap for coverage of contracture release in patients with normal skin around the cervical contracture area. However, in this study, patients did not have adequate normal skin for TE insertion or declined it.

In addition to delaying skin graft, we also used a software to determine corrections using quantitative measurements. The CMA was the first and the other was Legan's angle. Both angles were measured using the Digimizer Software. The normal cervico-mental angle is 121° for men and 126° for women, along with the mean Legan's angle of 12° (8°-16°) in normal cases.11,17 The median CMA in our patients was 172.71° (range, 156.40° to 178.37°) that decreased to 130.37° (range,109.97° to 142.06°) post-operatively. Indeed, correction of CMA in some patients in our study was not complete, but significant improvement was observed (P=0.012). However, decreasing Legan's angle from 13.99° (range, 12.32° to 24.17°) to 10.51° (range, 8.24° to 11.68°) visibly revealed its normalization and near adequate chin augmentation (P=0.012). The results were acceptable and patient's satisfaction during the last physical examination and at the 6-month follow-up were good and excellent, respectively. Motamed et al. reported excellent results in their case series by using this technique.7 Manafi et al. reported excellent and good results in 32 out of 34 burn patients with retrogenia, due to hypertrophic scar of the cervico-mental area by repositioning of fat tissues from labio-mental and cervico-mental areas to augment more chin prominence.2 Furthermore, quantity measurement using the Digimizer Software for CMA correction and chin advancement was not previously performed in similar published studies.2,7 Quantitative measurement for improving indexes may appear unnecessary; however, changes in the photographs pre- and post-operatively reveals the improvement in appearance. Notably, the quantitative tools will be required to compare the different techniques used for such reconstruction and evaluate the degree of improvement. Under other circumstances, quantitative measurement is superior to qualitative evaluation.

Although several well-known methods for chin augmentation are available, transposition of tissues appears to be a cost-effective alternative for addressing all the problems through structural rearrangement in patients with obtusity caused by hypertrophic scar in the cervico-mental area with microgenia and retrogenia

sec.title: Acknowledgment
sec.p: The authors would like to thank Amir Elhaei, PhD Student of Biostatistics, for his assistance in statical consulting and the Clinical Research and Development Unit of Bu-Ali Sina and Clinical Research and Development Unit of Imam Hospital, Mazandaran University of Medical Sciences for their support and cooperation throughout the study.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: Approved by Mazandaran University of Medical Science Research Ethical Committee (IR.MAZUMS.REC.1403.20418).

sec.title: Photo Consent
sec.p: Written consent has been received from the patients for photo publication

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jpra.2025.02.008.
